title: Why US plans for power-sharing in Libya will not work
gist: Chatham House argues that the US push, led by Trump adviser Massad Boulos, to reunify Libya via power-sharing between the Haftar and Dabaiba families will fail because it formalizes elite rent-seeking without fixing governance. Anti-corruption reports show a $9bn+ fuel-import subsidy bill and captured medicine supply chains that a unity deal would likely worsen, deterring the oil investment the plan aims to unlock.

insights.claim: Trump adviser Massad Boulos is pushing to break Libya's political deadlock by merging the two rival administrations, the Tripoli-based GNU controlled by the Dabaiba family and the Benghazi-based GNS aligned with Khalifa Haftar's LAAF, into a single unified government.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Watch Boulos's shuttle diplomacy as the key catalyst for any Libyan reunification and the oil-sector opening tied to it.
insights.quote: The US plan is to bring the Haftars and the Dabaibas together with other key constituencies and form a unified government.

insights.claim: LAAF deputy commander Saddam Haftar travelled to Washington in June to meet US Secretary of State Marco Rubio, signaling the Haftar family is engaging on power-sharing, while the Dabaiba family declined to travel but remains a target for the deal.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Haftar-side buy-in is advancing faster than Dabaiba engagement, so the Tripoli faction's willingness is the swing variable for a deal.
insights.locator: In June, LAAF deputy commander Saddam Haftar

insights.claim: Critics see the proposed Libyan unity deal as a return of family rule that legitimizes two rival dynasties while focusing on elite power-sharing rather than delivering public services to ordinary citizens.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Domestic opposition raises the risk that any signed deal lacks legitimacy and fails to hold, keeping political risk elevated.
insights.quote: it looks like the return of family rule and a way to legitimize the two rival families

insights.claim: Libya's fuel-import subsidy bill exceeded $9 billion by 2024, roughly $1,200 per person, with import volumes more than doubling since 2021 in an increase unjustifiable by any reasonable market demand.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The ballooning subsidy quantifies the fiscal drain and black-market leakage a naive unity deal would entrench rather than curb.
insights.quote: the import bill had reached over $9 billion by 2024, the equivalent of nearly $1,200 per person in Libya
insights.locator: The provision of fuel has been a major source of controversy

insights.claim: Libyan state-affiliated armed groups increased diesel consumption by 1,527 per cent in 2024 versus 2021 and the Tripoli South Power Plant by 1,368 per cent, indicating fuel is being diverted to black markets or smuggled internationally.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The scale of diversion shows vested armed interests capture the subsidy, a structural constraint on any governance reform.
insights.quote: Libya's patchwork of state-affiliated armed groups increased their diesel consumption by 1,527 per cent in 2024 compared to 2021, while consumption at Tripoli South Power Plant increased by 1,368 per cent.
insights.locator: Assessment of demand for fuel was found to lack a 'scientific' basis

insights.claim: Libya's roster of fuel-import suppliers was cut from 17 in 2021 to just six mostly newly created foreign companies in 2024, and a 450 per cent rise in the diesel CIF premium produced a near $600 million loss in that year alone.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Supplier concentration and an international beneficiary dimension mean reform must target foreign intermediaries, not just Libyan elites.
insights.quote: the increase in the cost, insurance and freight premium for diesel increased by 450 per cent, resulting in a near $600 million loss in 2024 alone
insights.locator: The number of suppliers of imported fuels was cut from 17

insights.claim: A 2022 change in leadership at Libya's National Oil Corporation, brokered between the Haftar and Dabaiba families by the UAE, led to a rapid expansion in fuel procurement despite the country's chronic refining deficit.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The UAE's brokering role shows external powers already shape Libya's oil rents, a dynamic that will persist under any US-brokered settlement.
insights.locator: In 2022, a change in leadership of the National Oil Corporation

insights.claim: Libya has no national framework regulating medicine supply, estimates needs via a 'speculative' methodology, and a few pharmaceutical companies with direct ties to state officials and members of parliament have grown their profit and market share exponentially in a clear conflict of interest.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Officials shaping procurement while owning the beneficiary firms shows conflict-of-interest capture spans beyond fuel, reinforcing that governance is the binding constraint.
insights.quote: Some were found to have direct ties to state officials and members of parliament.
insights.locator: The report on the supply of medicines

insights.claim: The US aims to scale up investment in Libya's oil sector if the two rival factions unify, but Chatham House argues power-sharing could worsen corruption by removing existing constraints on rising state spending.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The core investment thesis behind the deal may be self-defeating; expect the oil-sector upside to underdeliver absent governance conditionality.
insights.quote: Power-sharing might simply make corruption worse by reducing existing constraints on rising state expenditure.
insights.locator: So, how is this relevant to attempts to form a unified government?

insights.claim: International oil companies are reluctant to make significant capital investments in a Libyan market where legal enforcement depends on the whim of feuding ruling families whose competition would likely continue inside any new unified government.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Weak legal enforcement means IOC capex will stay on the sidelines even after a deal, capping realistic upside for Libyan oil exposure.
insights.quote: who are reluctant to make significant capital investments in a market where legal enforcement comes at the whim of feuding ruling families
insights.locator: It would also not be a positive outcome for international oil companies

insights.claim: A US-mediated unified budget for Libya agreed in April had not been meaningfully implemented, showing that political breakthroughs without an implementation plan do not change realities on the ground.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The stalled April budget is a leading indicator that headline unity announcements will not translate into on-the-ground change or investable stability.
insights.quote: political breakthroughs without an implementation plan do not change realities on the ground
insights.locator: The fact that a US-mediated unified budget for Libya agreed in April
